title: Dump Pipeline
rules: separate raw extraction from formatted output
avoid mixing runtime logic into dump generation
procedure: Store outputs for later lookup and comparison.

primary_when: the request is about generating a dump or structured export
the request is about repeatable assembly snapshots
the request is about turning raw assembly data into stable dump output
must_not_own_when: the request is mainly about runtime integration
the request is mainly about Harmony patching

solves: making reverse engineering output repeatable and comparable
turning raw assembly reading into structured files that can be searched and diffed
preserving enough evidence for later patch, probe, and facade design
does_not_solve: semantic validation in the running game
feature architecture outside the reverse-engineering handoff

core_principles: A dump pipeline should be deterministic before it is clever.
Raw extraction, normalization, formatting, and interpretation should remain separate stages.
Repeated dumps must be comparable across game versions.
Dump output should preserve original structure while adding indexes for practical lookup.
The best dump output supports later runtime contracts without becoming runtime code.
constraints: Do not mix Harmony patch logic into dump generation.
Do not drop unresolved references silently.
Do not reorder output unpredictably between runs.
Preserve signatures and metadata needed to identify target methods later.
Keep generated files inspectable by humans and searchable by tools.
decision_rules: Use dump-pipeline when the user needs repeatable exports or version diffs, not one-off inspection.
Attach assembly-reading and metadata-parsing before formatting decisions.
If the dump will feed modding work, include hook-candidate and reflection-candidate indexes.
If output volume is large, prefer one file per type or namespace plus summary indexes.
If a decompiler fails, emit an explicit failure record instead of inventing source.

preferred_patterns: stable file layout keyed by assembly, namespace, and type
separate raw member inventory and semantic candidate indexes
diff-friendly ordering and explicit unresolved-reference records
avoid_patterns: single monolithic dump file for a large game assembly
manual edits to generated dump output treated as source of truth
formatting that hides signatures, overloads, or declaring type context
runtime integration decisions embedded inside dump generator code

recipes.name: Create a comparable game assembly dump
recipes.when: Use when a modding project needs a repeatable snapshot of game internals.
steps: Read assemblies and build a raw model of modules, types, members, signatures, attributes, and references.
Normalize ordering without changing names or semantics.
Emit per-type or per-namespace records plus searchable indexes.
Emit unresolved and decompiler-failure records explicitly.
Generate optional candidate indexes for lifecycle hooks, UI contexts, network systems, and reflected state.
Keep the dump output separate from runtime integration code.

recipes.name: Compare dumps after a game update
recipes.when: Use when hooks, probes, or facades may have broken after a game version change.
steps: Generate the new dump with the same output layout and ordering as the previous version.
Diff candidate hook targets by declaring type, member name, signature, and visibility.
Diff reflected fields and properties used by probe adapters.
Mark removed, renamed, overloaded, or signature-changed members as integration risks.
Hand risk records to integration-surface-mapping before changing runtime code.

pitfalls: Generating output that cannot be diffed reliably after a game update.
Losing metadata tokens or signatures that are needed to re-identify targets.
Treating generated decompiled code as maintainable source.
Hiding failed member extraction behind an empty section.
Letting dump tooling decide runtime patch policy.
review_checklist: Is output deterministic between identical runs?
Are raw extraction and interpretation separated?
Can exact target signatures be recovered from the dump?
Are unresolved references and failed decompilation visible?
Does the dump support both search and version comparison?

references.title: Dump Pipeline
references.interpretation: Use this first for repeatable dump structure and runtime handoff guidance.
references.why: It defines the practical output shape for reverse-engineering artifacts.

references.title: Reverse Integration Map
references.interpretation: Use when dump output must feed modding hooks, probes, and facades.
references.why: It connects dump artifacts to runtime integration decisions.

references.title: Integration Surface Mapping
references.interpretation: Use when dump diffs need to become runtime risk or migration decisions.
references.why: It classifies changed members into hook, probe, facade, and rejected surfaces.
